figures.gloss: The mythic, primordial ancestors of the Marind-Anim who roamed the earth in the dream-time. They possessed extraordinary powers and could shape-shift between human, plant, and animal forms. Through their violent deaths and dismemberments, they birthed the staple crops, totems, and human lineages.
figures.facet: They are not worshipped as distant gods but are actively incarnated and physically re-enacted during secret initiation cults. Their mythic dismemberment is treated as the literal biological origin of sago and coconut, grounding cosmology in visceral anatomy.
figures.provenance: Jan van Baal's 'Dema: Description and Analysis of Marind-Anim Culture' (1966).

figures.gloss: Towering wooden effigies carved by the Asmat from inverted mangrove trees. Each pole embodies specific named ancestors who died through violence or sorcery, erected during elaborate feasts to promise vengeance and ensure the spirits' safe passage to Safan, the realm of the dead.
figures.facet: The carving of the pole is symbolically equated to headhunting; the felled tree bleeds sap like a severed human neck. It acts as a temporary vessel that must eventually be left to rot in the sago swamps, transferring ancestral power into the crops.
figures.provenance: Tobias Schneebaum's 'Asmat Images' (1985) and Adrian Gerbrands' fieldwork on Asmat woodcarving.

figures.gloss: The highly formalized system of competitive gift exchange in the New Guinea Highlands, centered on pigs, pearl shells, and wealth. It is a cosmic economy where 'Big Men' distribute wealth to create binding social debt, pacify enemies, and appease ancestral ghosts.
figures.facet: Wealth is not hoarded but aggressively given away. Prestige and spiritual power accrue only to the one who can overwhelm their rivals with unpayable generosity, transforming the biological surplus of pigs into sociopolitical immortality.
figures.provenance: Andrew Strathern's 'The Rope of Moka' (1971); Paul Wiessner's ethnohistory of the Enga Tee.

figures.gloss: The Asmat realm of the dead, located across the sea toward the setting sun. Ancestors must journey there in ghost canoes after their deaths have been avenged, settling into a parallel village life mirroring the living world.
figures.facet: It is not a realm of moral judgment but a strict reflection of the living world that demands equilibrium. Spirits cannot enter Safan if they carry unavenged grievance; they linger to plague the living until the headhunting or bisj obligation is met.
figures.provenance: Asmat mythological cycles recorded by Gerard Zegwaard (1959).

figures.gloss: The supreme creator and culture heroine of the Mountain Ok (Min) peoples. A giantess who traversed the mountains, building the first cult houses, establishing secret initiations, and distributing taro, pigs, and cowrie shells from her bilum (string bag).
figures.facet: Unlike typical withdrawn creators, Afek established a sacred geography of cult houses that remains physically maintained today. She deliberately split the world into male (ritual secrecy) and female (growth and fertility) spheres, trading roles with her brother to cement the division.
figures.provenance: Dan Jorgensen's 'Taro and Arrows' (1981); Robert Brumbaugh on Telefolmin mythology.

figures.gloss: The pervasive force of malevolent witchcraft and sorcery across New Guinea. It is the primary metaphysical explanation for disease, sudden death, and misfortune, often conceptualized as an invisible consumption of the victim's vital organs or soul.
figures.facet: Death is almost never considered 'natural'—it is an intentional act of malice requiring investigation, divination, and retribution. Sorcery acts as the dark, covert twin to the public, prestige-building exchanges of the Moka.
figures.provenance: Michele Stephen's 'Sorcerer and Witch in Melanesia' (1987).

figures.gloss: The active, lingering spirit of the recent dead. Across Papuan cultures, these ghosts inhabit the immediate environment, demanding pork sacrifices and strict adherence to custom. They enforce morality by striking the living with illness if neglected.
figures.facet: They are intimately domestic yet utterly terrifying. They are not distant deities to be passively worshipped, but jealous relatives who must be constantly managed, fed, and eventually driven away or transformed into generalized ancestral power.
figures.provenance: Roy Rappaport's 'Pigs for the Ancestors' (1968); Peter Lawrence's studies on highland religions.

figures.gloss: A colossal, phallic Dema-deity of the Marind and Trans-Fly region. He emerges annually from the east, marching westward, demanding rituals of male initiation to pass on vital life-force to the youth.
figures.facet: He embodies the absolute biological literalism of traditional Papuan ritual: spiritual growth is achieved through the physical transmission of bodily fluids from older males to initiates, establishing a lineage of substance that bypasses female reproduction.
figures.provenance: Jan van Baal (1966); Bruce Knauft's 'South Coast New Guinea Cultures' (1993).

figures.gloss: The Sepik men's cult of graded initiations centered on the haus tambaran (spirit house), whose secret voices — flutes and bullroarers sounded out of sight — are presented to women and uninitiated novices as the tambaran spirits themselves speaking.
figures.facet: The region's most famous religious institution turns on a managed open secret: full male initiates know the sounds are instruments, and the cult's power lies in maintaining the fiction across the gender line, not in any hidden metaphysical truth.
figures.provenance: Donald Tuzin, The Voice of the Tambaran (1980).

figures.gloss: A Middle Sepik (Iatmul, Chambri) initiation in which hundreds of small cuts are made across an initiate's back and shoulders, raised afterward into crocodile-skin welts, rebirthing the young man from the body of the ancestral crocodile.
figures.facet: The founding myth is written directly onto the body rather than merely narrated: the initiate does not hear that he descends from the crocodile, he is made to carry its skin for the rest of his life.
figures.provenance: Gregory Bateson's Iatmul fieldwork region, Naven (1936); subsequent Sepik ethnography on the tambaran cult's initiatory grades.

figures.gloss: Trobriand spirits of the dead who dwell on Tuma, an island to the northwest, and return to their home villages each year at the milamala harvest festival to be feasted and honored before departing again.
figures.facet: The lowland-island counterpart to the roster's highland Ancestral Ghost: where the highland ghost is a constant, jealous domestic presence, the Baloma's contact with the living is calendrical and festive, confined to a single annual homecoming.
figures.provenance: Bronisław Malinowski, 'Baloma: The Spirits of the Dead in the Trobriand Islands', Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute (1916).

figures.gloss: The ceremonial inter-island exchange ring of the Massim archipelago, in which soulava shell necklaces circulate clockwise and mwali armshells circulate counter-clockwise among partners bound for life, ranking men by the fame of the valuables that briefly pass through their hands.
figures.facet: The Papuan region's other great exchange cosmology beside the Moka: where the Moka is a competitive, debt-generating contest between rival Big Men, the Kula is a non-competitive, lifelong partnership circuit — two opposite social logics both organized around the ritual movement of wealth.
figures.provenance: Bronisław Malinowski, Argonauts of the Western Pacific (1922).

figures.gloss: The Elema (Papuan Gulf) ceremonial cycle in which towering hevehe masks, understood as daughters of the sea-spirits, are built inside the men's house over as long as twenty years before finally emerging to dance publicly and then being destroyed.
figures.facet: One of the most elaborate ritual cycles ever recorded turns entirely on delayed gratification: decades of secret, graded construction culminate in a single public appearance whose whole meaning is that the object is then deliberately destroyed.
figures.provenance: F. E. Williams, Drama of Orokolo: The Social and Ceremonial Life of the Elema (1940).

figures.gloss: In Namau and neighboring Papuan Gulf usage, the pervasive life-force or soul-stuff residing in persons, masks, and ritual objects, on whose presence and strength their spiritual efficacy depends.
figures.facet: An early datum in the comparative anthropological study of impersonal sacred power: efficacy here is not the gift of a personal god but the measurable presence or depletion of a substance-like force in a person or thing.
figures.provenance: J. H. Holmes, In Primitive New Guinea (1924).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: Pigs are raised in the physical world to be sacrificed in the Moka, satisfying the ancestral ghosts who in return guarantee the fertility of the next generation.
Intra-tradition: The pole is the ritual vehicle and proxy; Safan is the cosmological destination it guarantees for the deceased.
Intra-tradition: Moka is the public, honorable exchange of wealth that builds society; Suangi is the hidden, malicious theft of life-force that destroys it.
Intra-tradition: Sosom is the greatest and most terrifying of the Dema, actively enforcing the initiatory laws the ancestral Dema established.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Dismembered divine ancestors whose physical bodies are transformed into the foundational agricultural staples of their people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Primordial beings whose bodily sacrifice directly constructs the physical universe, flora, fauna, and human social categories.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The towering cosmic tree acting as an axis mundi, though the Asmat pole is ritually felled and inverted to transport souls rather than permanently standing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A great tree acting as the blood-drenched conduit bridging the living and the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems equate massive, performative wealth distribution and intentional debt creation with spiritual authority and cosmic equilibrium.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive, highly synchronized inter-village ritual cycle that temporarily resets social debts and passes generational authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A dark realm of the dead located across a treacherous body of water, where ancestors reside in a shadowy mirror of the living world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme divine woman who creates the people and dictates their foundational rituals, sustenance, and architectural dwellings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial female creator who birthed the cosmos and distributed its governance into dualistic spheres.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ancient grandmother goddess who provides the foundational elements of civilization, fertility, and animal husbandry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An omnipresent, invisible force of pure malice held strictly responsible for all death, disease, and misfortune in an otherwise ordered world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Blood-thirsty, malicious forces and dark sorceries that invisibly consume the life force of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Localized, ever-present ancestors who demand literal sustenance (meat/libations) and fiercely police the moral boundaries of their descendants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The immediate family dead who maintain heavy, jealous involvement in their descendants' affairs and require consistent animal sacrifice.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A towering, terrifying deity who arrives from the outside, demanding ecstatic initiation, bodily surrender, and the transmission of vital fluids.

Intra-tradition: two regionally distinct male-cult complexes (Sepik Tambaran, Trans-Fly Sosom) that both demand graded initiation and the transmission of vital force from older men to youths, without direct historical connection between the two regions.
Intra-tradition: crocodile scarification is the bodily ordeal performed within the Iatmul tambaran cult's initiatory grades, documented together in the same Sepik ethnographic record.

Intra-tradition: the lowland-island Trobriand dead and the highland ancestral ghosts are both actively managed, honor-demanding presences, differing chiefly in the rhythm of contact — annual festival versus constant domestic vigilance.
Intra-tradition: the two great Papuan exchange cosmologies, ring-partnership versus competitive contest, both converting circulating wealth into social and spiritual standing.

Intra-tradition: two lowland regions (Papuan Gulf, Asmat) that each invest years of graded, secret craft into towering carved ritual effigies which are then deliberately allowed to be destroyed or left to rot, transferring their power onward rather than being preserved.
Intra-tradition: Suangi sorcery is described as an invisible consumption of exactly the imunu life-force that gives a person vitality — the concept and the malevolent force that preys on it.
Cross-tradition: unrelated graded men's/medicine societies that transmit secret ritual knowledge through successive initiatory ranks, structurally parallel with no claimed contact.
Cross-tradition: both are closed male initiatory societies whose masked or voiced spirit-presences are in fact managed secrets known only to the initiated, kept from women and the uninitiated.

Cross-tradition: island Melanesian and Massim ancestor-spirits that intervene in the living world and require ongoing propitiation, independently attested regional variants of the same broad Melanesian ancestor complex.

Cross-tradition: masked ceremonial society complexes elsewhere in Melanesia (the Banks Islands' Tamate societies) that parallel the Papuan Gulf's mask-cult structure of secret construction followed by public appearance.

Cross-tradition: both are early ethnographic terms (Holmes on imunu, Codrington on mana) for an impersonal, quantifiable sacred force pervading persons and objects; independently documented regional concepts rather than a single diffused term.

dossier.essence: Not a single religion but the many indigenous religions of New Guinea — highland and lowland, Papuan- and Austronesian-speaking — one of the most linguistically and ritually diverse regions on earth. Across that diversity certain family features recur: intimate ancestral ghosts, the explanation of death by sorcery or witchcraft, secret male initiation cults, and, in the highlands, competitive pig-and-shell exchange. These remain living traditions, though much reshaped by a century of Christian missions.
dossier.cosmos: Typically there is no remote high god; the cosmos is filled instead with ancestral ghosts and place-spirits enmeshed in daily life. Among the Marind-anim the primordial 'dema' beings, killed and dismembered in the mythic time, became the crops, totems, and human groups; the land of the dead often lies across water or toward the setting sun (as in the Asmat Safan). Death is seldom deemed 'natural' — it is charged to sorcery (sanguma/suangi), demanding divination and redress.
dossier.practice: Ritual life centers on propitiating ancestral ghosts with pork; on secret male initiation using bullroarers and sacred flutes, seclusion, and (in societies such as the Sambia and Marind) ritualized transfers of male substance held to 'grow' boys; on mortuary rites; and, among the Asmat, on headhunting and bisj-pole feasts. In the highlands the great ceremonial exchange cycles (the Enga Tee, the Melpa Moka) let 'big-men' win prestige by giving away pigs and pearl-shells, while sorcery and counter-sorcery pervade everything.
dossier.canon: Oral — myth cycles, initiation lore, spells, and ritual oratory — with no scripture; the tradition is known through a large 20th-century ethnographic record rather than from sacred texts.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the fusion of religion with exchange and reciprocity — a single economy of life-force in which ancestral debt, competitive gift-giving, and the ever-present threat of sorcery are the ritual regulation of life itself.
sources: Jan van Baal, 'Dema: Description and Analysis of Marind-anim Culture' (1966)
Roy A. Rappaport, 'Pigs for the Ancestors: Ritual in the Ecology of a New Guinea People' (1968)
Gilbert H. Herdt, 'Guardians of the Flutes: Idioms of Masculinity' (1981)
